grammar.meaning: Identifies something as belonging to a larger category or group; means '~ is one of ~'.
grammar.explanation: Used to classify or categorize something as part of a larger set. The first part (～) is the specific thing, the second part (～の一つ) is the broader category. Common in explanations and definitions.

examples.japanese: 犬は動物の一つです。
examples.reading: いぬはどうぶつのひとつです。
examples.english: A dog is one of the animals. / A dog is a type of animal.

examples.japanese: 日本語は言語の一つです。
examples.reading: にほんごはげんごのひとつです。
examples.english: Japanese is one of the languages. / Japanese is a language.

examples.japanese: 寿司は日本料理の一つです。
examples.reading: すしはにほんりょうりのひとつです。
examples.english: Sushi is one of the Japanese dishes. / Sushi is a type of Japanese food.

maynardRef.title: Being: です, だ, ある, いる
maynardRef.excerpt:
82 CHAPTER 2. VERB GRAMMAR
of course also be used to describe the process of "becoming'" itself, (such
as "to quickly become [..]") by using proper adverbs.
The verb なる is a 五段 verb, without any irregularities, meaning its
bases areなら,なり，なる,なれ andなれ.
While typically used in combination with the verb particle lC, it can
also be used in combination with the particle と, in which case its meaning
changes from "become'" to "be"(often interpreted as the immediate future
"will be"). This can be illustr

examples.japanese: The verb なる is a 五段 verb, without any irregularities, meaning its
bases areなら,なり，なる,なれ andなれ.
examples.english: While typically used in combination with the verb particle lC, it can

examples.japanese: also be used in combination with the particle と, in which case its meaning
examples.english: changes from "become'" to "be"(often interpreted as the immediate future

grammar.pattern: とかんがえられている
grammar.meaning: Is considered, Is thought of as

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

maynardRef.title: Continuative: て form
maynardRef.excerpt:
3.2. FURTHER INFLECTIONS 103
3.2.2 Continuative: て form
The continuative in Japanese is also commonly refered to as the "て form',
because it relies on the 連用形 of classical helper verb for completion, つ,
which is て. This inflection is used for at least three things in Japanese,
of which verb chaining is probably the most frequently used. Aside from
this, it is also used to issue (implied) imperatives, and it can be used in
combination with several special verbs to create special constructions.
ba

examples.japanese: 3.2.2 Continuative: て form
The continuative in Japanese is also commonly refered to as the "て form',
because it relies on the 連用形 of classical helper verb for completion, つ,

grammar.meaning: Attached to a verb stem, なおす means to do something again properly or to correct it.
grammar.explanation: なおす (直す) as a suffix attaches to the conjunctive form (ます-stem) of a verb to mean 'redo' or 'do something again from scratch, correctly.' It implies the first attempt was flawed or incomplete and must be redone. It is neutral in register and used in both spoken and written Japanese.

examples.japanese: レポートを書きなおしました。
examples.reading: レポートをかきなおしました。
examples.english: I rewrote the report.

examples.japanese: 間違えたので、最初からやりなおしてください。
examples.reading: まちがえたので、さいしょからやりなおしてください。
examples.english: You made a mistake, so please start over from the beginning.

examples.japanese: この文章を読みなおしたら、意味がわかりました。
examples.reading: このぶんしょうをよみなおしたら、いみがわかりました。
examples.english: When I reread this sentence, I understood the meaning.

grammar.meaning: Expresses intensity or exactness; means 'completely', 'entirely', 'bright', or 'right' depending on context.
grammar.explanation: 真 by itself or 真っ (with certain adjectives like colors and times) emphasizes a quality to the fullest. Common before night (真夜中), colors (真っ白, 真っ赤), and to show completeness. Neutral register.

examples.japanese: 真っ白な雪が降っています。
examples.reading: まっしろなゆきがふっています。
examples.english: Pure white snow is falling. / Completely white snow is falling.

examples.japanese: 真夜中に起きました。
examples.reading: まよなかにおきました。
examples.english: I woke up at midnight. / I woke up in the middle of the night.

examples.japanese: その花は真っ赤です。
examples.reading: そのはなはまっかです。
examples.english: That flower is bright red. / That flower is completely red.

grammar.explanation: ほかに and ほか are used to list additional items, alternatives, or things besides what has been mentioned. ほかに(も) adds an additional item; ほか(に)は contrasts items or sets up a new topic. This expression is essential for making lists and comparisons.

examples.japanese: ほかに何かありますか。
examples.reading: ほかになにかありますか。
examples.english: Is there anything else?

examples.japanese: コーヒーのほかに、紅茶もあります。
examples.reading: こーひーのほかに、こうちゃもあります。
examples.english: Besides coffee, we also have tea.

examples.japanese: 日本語のほかに、韓国語も勉強しています。
examples.reading: にほんごのほかに、かんこくごもべんきょうしています。
examples.english: Besides Japanese, I'm also studying Korean.

grammar.pattern: でできる・からできる
grammar.meaning: Indicates what something is made from (material or origin).
grammar.explanation: This pattern shows the material or origin of something. で indicates the material or substance (e.g., wood, metal), while から indicates the source or origin (where something comes from). Both are used with できている or できています to show composition.

examples.japanese: このテーブルは木でできています。
examples.reading: このてーぶるはきでできています。
examples.english: This table is made of wood.

examples.japanese: このジュースはぶどうからできています。
examples.reading: このじゅーすはぶどうからできています。
examples.english: This juice is made from grapes.

examples.japanese: このセーターは羊毛でできています。
examples.reading: このせーたーはようもうでできています。
examples.english: This sweater is made of wool.

grammar.meaning: The question form of a counter (何 + counter + か) means 'some' or 'several' without specifying exact number.
grammar.explanation: This pattern combines 何 (what/some) with a counter and か to indicate an unspecified but small quantity. Used when the exact number is unclear or unimportant. Common in both spoken and written Japanese.

examples.japanese: 何人か来ました。
examples.reading: なんにんかきました。
examples.english: Several people came. / Some people came.

examples.japanese: 何冊か本を読みました。
examples.reading: なんさつかほんをよみました。
examples.english: I read several books. / I read a few books.

examples.japanese: 何日かかかります。
examples.reading: なんにちかかかります。
examples.english: It will take several days. / It takes a few days.

grammar.meaning: Used to express that something can be seen, observed, or witnessed
grammar.explanation: がみられる is a potential passive form expressing that something is observable or can be witnessed. The subject marked by が is what can be seen or observed. This form is often used in academic, scientific, or formal contexts to describe observable phenomena or trends.

examples.japanese: ここではいろいろな野鳥が見られます。
examples.reading: ここではいろいろなやちょうがみられます。
examples.english: Various wild birds can be seen here.

examples.japanese: 最近、新しい技術の発展が見られています。
examples.reading: さいきん、あたらしいぎじゅつのはってんがみられています。
examples.english: Recently, the development of new technology can be observed.

examples.japanese: その地域では大きな変化が見られました。
examples.reading: そのちいきではおおきなへんかがみられました。
examples.english: In that region, a major change was observed.

maynardRef.title: Passive: れる/られる
maynardRef.excerpt:
134 CHAPTER 3. MORE GRAMMAR
3.2.11 Passive:れる/られる
Passive constructions are, as their name implies, constructions which de-
scribe actions in a passive voice. Unlike "I eat dinner" or "The cat is play-
ing with the squeaky toy'", which are in active voice, they refer to phrases
like "Dinner was eaten by me'" or "The squeaky toy was played with by
うけみ
the cat". In Japanese, this passive voice, called 受身, is achieved through
the use of the two helper verbs れる (for 五段 verbs) and られる (for 一段
verbs),

examples.japanese: 3.2.11 Passive:れる/られる
examples.english: Passive constructions are, as their name implies, constructions which de-

examples.japanese: the cat". In Japanese, this passive voice, called 受身, is achieved through
the use of the two helper verbs れる (for 五段 verbs) and られる (for 一段

grammar.meaning: Almost every ~, Nearly every ~, On a ~ basis
grammar.explanation: Indicating that something happens almost every time.

examples.japanese: 毎日のように雨が降っている。
examples.reading: まいにちのようにあめがふっている。
examples.english: It's raining almost every day.

examples.japanese: 毎週のように新しい製品が発表される。
examples.reading: まいしゅうのようにあたらしいせいひんがはっぴょうされる。
examples.english: New products are announced almost weekly.

examples.japanese: 毎月のように価格が変わる。
examples.reading: まいげつのようにかかくがかわる。
examples.english: The price changes almost monthly.

grammar.meaning: Used to express the purpose, intent, or desired result of an action or change
grammar.explanation: ように indicates what someone is trying to achieve or what result they hope for. It can follow action verbs to show purpose or state verbs to show desired result. The main verb determines the politeness level.

examples.japanese: 毎日運動するようにしています。
examples.reading: まいにちうんどうするようにしています。
examples.english: I'm trying to exercise every day.

examples.japanese: 時間に遅れないようにしてください。
examples.reading: じかんにおくれないようにしてください。
examples.english: Please make sure you're not late.

examples.japanese: 雨が降らないように祈っています。
examples.reading: あめがふらないようにいのっています。
examples.english: I'm praying that it won't rain.

grammar.meaning: A construction that explains reasons, contrasts, or provides context in a gentle or polite way.
grammar.explanation: んだけど and んですが are explanatory particles combined with conjunctions, used to softly introduce reasons or mild contradictions. んです adds emphasis or explanation, and けど/が add a 'but' or 'and' nuance. This is common in conversational Japanese for natural dialogue at the A2 level.

examples.japanese: 実は、昨日は疲れていたんですが、今日は大丈夫です。
examples.reading: じつは、きのう は つかれて いた んです が、きょう は だいじょうぶ です。
examples.english: Actually, I was tired yesterday, but I'm fine today.

examples.japanese: 申し訳ないんですが、明日は来られないんです。
examples.reading: もうしわけ ない んです が、あした は こられない んです。
examples.english: I'm sorry, but I can't come tomorrow.

examples.japanese: このレストランは安いんですが、とても美味しいんです。
examples.reading: この レストラン は やすい んです が、とても おいしい んです。
examples.english: This restaurant is inexpensive, and it's really delicious.

maynardRef.title: Being: です, だ, ある, いる
maynardRef.excerpt:
82 CHAPTER 2. VERB GRAMMAR
of course also be used to describe the process of "becoming'" itself, (such
as "to quickly become [..]") by using proper adverbs.
The verb なる is a 五段 verb, without any irregularities, meaning its
bases areなら,なり，なる,なれ andなれ.
While typically used in combination with the verb particle lC, it can
also be used in combination with the particle と, in which case its meaning
changes from "become'" to "be"(often interpreted as the immediate future
"will be"). This can be illustr

examples.japanese: The verb なる is a 五段 verb, without any irregularities, meaning its
bases areなら,なり，なる,なれ andなれ.
examples.english: While typically used in combination with the verb particle lC, it can

examples.japanese: also be used in combination with the particle と, in which case its meaning
examples.english: changes from "become'" to "be"(often interpreted as the immediate future

grammar.meaning: Because of that, So?, And then?, Therefore, With that

examples.japanese: 例文です。
examples.reading: れいぶんです。
examples.english: Example sentence.

grammar.meaning: When は follows a number or amount, it indicates 'at least that much' or an approximate quantity.
grammar.explanation: The particle は after a specific number or amount softens the exactness, suggesting approximation or 'at least' that amount. Common in casual speech to avoid sounding too precise. Neutral register.

examples.japanese: 100人は来ました。
examples.reading: ひゃくにんはきました。
examples.english: At least 100 people came. / Some 100 people came.

examples.japanese: 3時間はかかります。
examples.reading: さんじかんはかかります。
examples.english: It takes at least 3 hours.

examples.japanese: 1000円は必要です。
examples.reading: せんえんはひつようです。
examples.english: You need at least 1000 yen.

grammar.meaning: A verb-forming suffix meaning to transform something into a certain state or to make something become a certain way.
grammar.explanation: This suffix attaches to nouns to create verbs expressing transformation, modernization, or change. Common in both everyday speech and formal or technical contexts. Examples include 便利化 (to make convenient), 現実化 (to realize/make real), デジタル化 (to digitalize). Used to describe processes of change or transformation.

examples.japanese: 自分の考えを現実化しました。
examples.reading: じぶんのかんがえをげんじつかしました。
examples.english: I made my thoughts a reality.

examples.japanese: インターネットは生活を便利化しました。
examples.reading: いんたーねっとはせいかつをべんりかしました。
examples.english: The internet made life more convenient.

examples.japanese: この会社は最近、デジタル化を始めました。
examples.reading: このかいしゃはさいきん、でじたるかをはじめました。
examples.english: This company has recently begun its digital transformation.

maynardRef.title: Doing: する
maynardRef.excerpt:
84 CHAPTER 2. VERB GRAMMAR
2.5.31 Doing: する
We've already seen す る as irregular verb, and it has been used in enough
example sentences to let it be no surprise that it means "do'". However,
this isn't the only meaning for this verb. When used in combination with
a direct object – as transitive verb – す る does mean "do', but when used
as intransitive verb, its meaning should be considered to be "decide on'
or "choose'". To show this difference in meaning between the transitive
たどうし じどうし
(strictly

examples.japanese: 2.5.31 Doing: する
We've already seen す る as irregular verb, and it has been used in enough
examples.english: example sentences to let it be no surprise that it means "do'". However,

examples.japanese: a direct object – as transitive verb – す る does mean "do', but when used
examples.english: as intransitive verb, its meaning should be considered to be "decide on'

grammar.meaning: Used to describe something as characteristic of or typical of a person, thing, or group
grammar.explanation: This らしい expresses what is characteristic or typical of someone or something, differing from the hearsay or inference meaning. It creates adjective-like descriptions (e.g., 大人らしい = adult-like, typical of adults) and can be modified with negative forms.

examples.japanese: その態度は大人らしくありません。
examples.reading: そのたいどはおとならしくありません。
examples.english: That attitude is not characteristic of an adult.

examples.japanese: この洋服は女性らしいです。
examples.reading: このようふくはじょせいらしいです。
examples.english: This clothing is feminine / characteristic of women.

examples.japanese: 彼の意見は若者らしいですね。
examples.reading: かれのいけんはわかものらしいですね。
examples.english: His opinion is typical of young people, isn't it?

grammar.meaning: Expresses hope, prayer, or wish that something will happen or become true.
grammar.explanation: This pattern combines '～ように' (so that, in order that) with the verb 祈る (to pray), expressing fervent hope, prayer, or wishes for a desired outcome. Used when someone deeply hopes for or prays for something to happen. It conveys a sense of earnest desire or spiritual hope.

examples.japanese: 試験に合格するように祈っています。
examples.reading: しけんにごうかくするようにいのっています。
examples.english: I'm praying that I will pass the exam.

examples.japanese: 家族が幸せになるように祈ります。
examples.reading: かぞくがしあわせになるようにいのります。
examples.english: I pray that my family will be happy.

examples.japanese: 友達が早く元気になるように祈った。
examples.reading: ともだちがはやくげんきになるようにいのった。
examples.english: I prayed that my friend would recover quickly.

grammar.meaning: Quite a few; quite a lot; not a small number
grammar.explanation: This double negative pattern (少なくない = not few) actually means something is considerable or significant. It's used to indicate a substantial quantity of something without directly saying 'many' or 'a lot.' It softens the expression while still conveying a large amount.

examples.japanese: クラスには日本人の学生が少なくない。
examples.reading: クラスには にほんじんの がくせいが すくなくない。
examples.english: There are quite a few Japanese students in the class.

examples.japanese: 毎年、事故で亡くなる人は少なくない。
examples.reading: まいとし、 じこで なくなる ひとは すくなくない。
examples.english: Every year, quite a number of people die in accidents.

examples.japanese: この本には間違いが少なくない。
examples.reading: この ほんには まちがいが すくなくない。
examples.english: There are quite a few mistakes in this book.

maynardRef.title: Negative presence: ない
maynardRef.excerpt:
2.6. MORE VERB GRAMMAR 85
2.5.5 Negative presence: ない
I know that な W is not a verb, but the reason it's in this list is because of
ある， which is very much a verb. Since ある means "exist" for inanimate
things, and since the adjective tな means "not (exist)", there are actually
(almost) always two interpretations possible when な い is used in a sen-
tence, by virtue of ある having two possible interpretations:
ラジオがない。
1) "There is no radio."
2) "I do not have a radio."
So for this reason it has been in

examples.japanese: 2.5.5 Negative presence: ない
I know that な W is not a verb, but the reason it's in this list is because of
ある， which is very much a verb. Since ある means "exist" for inanimate

contentBlocks.title: Dialogue genki_2_9
contentBlocks.text:
You: can also express the idea of "can do"using a more complex construction: verb dictionary form
Mary: can play the guitar.
You: cannot keep dogs in this apartment.
As: we learned in Lesson 9, to give the reason for something, we can use the conjunction .
にほんご: にほんこ
じゆきょう: だいす
I: really like my Japanese class, because Japanese is interesting.
When: you want to mention not just one but two (or more) reasons, you can use L in place of
から: L usually follows a predicate in the short form.
にほんご: せんせい
わたし: にほんご
じゆきょう: だいす
I: really like my Japanese class, because Japanese is interesting, and our teacher is good.
A: 国に帰りたいですか。
くにか元: Do you want to go back home?
B: いいえ、日本の生活は楽しいし、いい友だちがいるし、帰りたくないです。
No: Life here in Japan is good, and I have good friends here, so I don't want to go back.
When: you use just one L clause, you imply that it is not the only reason for the situation.
まち: せいかつ
らく: Life in this city is easygoing. Things are inexpensive, for one thing.
When: you have an-taking verb in theときconstruction, you keep the original particle，unlike
with: the potential verbs.
In: the very polite speech style, L can also follow the long forms.
わたし: らいねん
にほ人: べ人う
にほん: I will study Japanese next year, too. I like Japan, and what is more, the Japanese language is interesting.
寸: にほん

contentBlocks.title: Dialogue genki_2_21
contentBlocks.text:
O: どろぼう Burglar
In: this lesson, we will.
Talk: about bad experiences
Tell: somebody what we wish them to do
Check: if things have been prepared
Report: incidents to police
会話: Dialo
John: runs into his landlady's house.k21-01/02
おおや: たいへん
はい: 2大家：
なに: ジョン：パソコンとスマホと……バイトでためたお金もないです。
かね: 大家：
けいさつ: れんらく
A: police officer comes to John's apartment.
警察: かはかけてあったですか。
ジョン: さあ……きのの夜は飲んで帰ってきたから、かをかけたかどうか
か元: よく覚えていないんです。
おぼ: 警察：
おぼ: ジョン：
しうてん: いちじはん
警察: どろぼうは、その後入ったんですね。
あとはい: 7ジョン：ええ。朝、部屋がめちゃくちだった
警察: 寝てい間にどぼに入られて、
ね: あいだ
はい: 気がつかなかったんですか。
き: 11ジョン：

contentBlocks.title: Dialogue 2_textbook_genki_2_21
contentBlocks.text:
たん: K21-08 (E-J)
NoUns: ちかん
criminal: どうりよう
同僚: colleague
business: meeting; conference
蚊: mosquito
ガソリン: gasoline
poster: もんく
文句: complaint
government: じぎようりよう
授業料: tuition
factory: かんきよう
環境: environment
last: train (of the day)
old: days; past
far: (away)
ひどい: awful
安全: safe
変: strange; unusual
to: put; to lay; to place
to: wrap;to cover(~を)
to: post; to stick(place l object)

contentBlocks.title: Dialogue 2_textbook_genki_2_21
contentBlocks.text:
文法: Grammar
ぼう: Passive Sentences
When: you are inconvenienced by something somebody else has done, you can express your dis-
satisfaction: using a passive sentence. Suppose, for example, that you were bothered by your friend's
unauthorized: use of your car. Compare (a) the objective description of the event and (b) the pas-
sive: version, which makes clear how you feel about it:
A: friend of mine used my car.
とも: くるま
つか: (b)私は友だちに
As: you can see from the above example, the basic makeup of a passive sentence is like the follow-
ing: examples.
Passive: sentence
私は: 友だちに
つか: (victim)は
I: had my car used by a friend.
Let: us first examine what the passive form of a verb looks like.
Passive: form
食べる: 食べられる
行く: 行かれる
死ぬ: 死なれる
泳がれる: 小遊買
話す: 話される
取られる: 買われる
くるこられる: するされる
With: the verbs that end with the hiragana , we see a "w" intervening, just as in the negative short forms.

contentBlocks.title: Dialogue 2_textbook_genki_2_21
contentBlocks.text:
さそ: I was asked out by that person for a date.
とも: しょうかい
I: was introduced by my big brother to a friend of his.
ひと: そんけい
That: person is looked up to by most everyone.
When: someone says these, they probably do not mean that they were inconvenienced by how
things: have turned out. There are relatively few verbs that come out neutral in their meaning when
they: are turned into the passive form.
You: can use the te-form of a verb + the helping verb あ る to characterize a situation that has been
brought: about on purpose by somebody who remains unnamed in the sentence.
さむ: The heater is on, because it is cold.
うえはな: Flowers are on the table.
There: is another type of passive sentence, with non-human subjects, which naturally lacks the implication that
the: inanimate, nonsentient subjects are inconvenienced. The passive sentences of this type are found more com-
monly: in the written language than in the spoken language. The type of passive sentences we learn in this lesson
is: called "affective passive," while the other type presented in this footnote is called "direct passive."
A: park will be built here.
The: picture was drawn by Picasso.
The: Olympic Games were not held that year.
As: you can see in the second example above, the human agent of the actions in such sentences is followed by l

contentBlocks.title: Dialogue 2_textbook_genki_2_21
contentBlocks.text:
Expression: Note
that: you are speaking from the point of view of a person who is at the destination
of: the return trip. Similarly, if you sayコ当き，youwill
be: saying either that you went out, bought food and came back to your apart-
by: at a convenience store.
かね: the verbある.The standard negative form ofあるisありません,while the sub-
standard: alternative form is built up of the short form negative な plus the po-
liteness: marker です.
More: generally, you may hear the long form alternative negative verbs made
up: of short form negative plus す.
standard: alternative
Present: 見えません
見えないです: Past

contentBlocks.title: Dialogue 2_textbook_genki_2_21
contentBlocks.text:
あやか: ちかん
おじさん: 友だち
ふる: さわる
抗: C. Pair Work—Discuss your bad experiences. Ask your partner the following questions,
using: the passive forms.
Example: 友だちが笑う

D: Pair Work—Your partner looks upset. Ask what the problem is. When you answer, use
the: passive form. Expand the conversation.
Example: A stranger punched you.
A: どうしたんですか。
B: 知らない人になぐられたんです。
ひと: A：それはひどいですね。警察に行ったほがいいですよ。
B: 大丈夫です。なぐられた後、私もその人をけりましたから。
だいじょうぶ: あとわたし
ひと: 1. Your roommate made the room dirty.
